title: practical english usage - Part 160
content:
Note also the structure with possessive 's.

She's the fastest runner of them all.  He's the best of the lot.

But of can be used before plurals, and before lot.

She's the fastest runner in the class, (     ... of the class.) not I'm the happiest man in the world, (     .. ,-ef the world.) place or group.

After superlatives, we  do not usually use of with a singular word referring to a 7 the    happiest          man     in   the    world She is older than I (am), (formal) She's older than me. (informal) formal style, subject pronouns (I, etc) are used (usually with verbs).

In an informal style, object pronouns (me, etc) are used after than. In a more 6 than     me;   than     I (am) grammar •  208  comparison: advanced points In this structure, the was originally a demonstrative, meaning 'by that’ .

say, for example, Those pills have made him  all the slimmer.

Note that this structure is used mainly to express abstract ideas. We   would not He explained it all carefully, but I was still none the wiser.

He didn't seem to be any the worse for his experience.

Any and none can be used in similar structures.

Her accident made it all the more important to get home fast.

I feel all the better for that swim.

'even more ...'.

All the + comparative (more common in British English) suggests the idea of 2  all/any/none           the    + comparative fat people.) less expensive clothes for the fuller figure (Compare cheap clothes for Comparatives are often used in advertising to make things sound less definite.

The cleverest students were two girls from York.

the slower learners.

There are two classes - one for the cleverer students and one for Compare: this, comparatives make a less clear and narrow selection than superlatives.

Comparatives can suggest ideas like 'relatively', ‘ more than average’ . Used like 1  comparative         meaning       'relatively',      'more    than    average' 208 c omparis     on:  a dvanced      poin   ts  __   __ For modification of too. ►    610.3.

This is your very last chance.

You're the very first person I've spoken to today.

Bring out your very best wine - Michael's coming to dinner.

Note the special use of very to emphasise superlatives and first, next and last.

4 very     with    superlatives This is easily the worst party I've been to this year.

I'm nearly the oldest in the company.

He's quite the most stupid man I've ever met. (BrE) She's by far the oldest. We're walking by far the slowest.

He's much  the most imaginative of them all. (BrE) and easily. Much  and quite are used in this way mostly in British English.

adverbs of degree such as quite (meaning 'absolutely'), almost, practically, nearly Superlative noun phrases can be modified by much  and by far, and by other 3 much,      by   far,    quite,      etc   with    superlative        noun    phrases far fewer words (mueh/many^fewer-words) Much  and many are not used to modify less or fewer, in plural noun phrases.

many /far / a lot, etc more opportunities much  / far / a lot, etc more money of much. - Of  all my friends, he’s (the) nicest. (comparing different people) person or thing in different situations. Compare: However, we  do not use the with superlatives when we  compare the same could find.) not This dictionary is the best I could find. (     This dictionary is best I The cannot be dropped when a superlative is used with a defining expression.

This dictionary is (the) best.

I’m  the greatest.  Which of the boys is (the) strongest?

sometimes dropped in an informal style.

After linking verbs, superlative adjectives also usually have the, though it is It’s the best book I’ve ever read.

Nouns with superlative adjectives normally have the article the.
